dialogue.utterance: Dispatch Unit 3 for a cardiac arrest at an apartment in [CITY].
Hello, this is Jasmine, an EMT with the rescue squad. I see the dispatcher mentioned this is a cardiac arrest call.
Yes, the patient was found unresponsive on the floor behind the bed.
Does he respond to any stimuli at all?
No.
Do you know if he responded to a voice or touch?
No, nothing.
We'll check his airway. Is there any possible airway obstruction?
I'm assessing for breathing now.
I'll check for pulses and signs of circulation.
No pulse detected.
There's no evidence of breathing initially, but now we detect 8 shallow breaths per minute.
EKG shows asystole.
Initiating CPR with the AutoPulse.
No blood pressure is registering on our monitor.
Oxygen saturation is not detected.
Blood glucose is initially not detectable, but later it rises to 449 mg/dL.
Administering 1 mg of epinephrine intraosseously now.
Securing an intraosseous access in preparation for repeated medication.
Is there any known medical history for the patient?
He had insulin-dependent diabetes, but we don't have full details.
Glucose levels are 449 mg/dL.
There's a 15 cm laceration on the right forearm with stitches.
Pupils are 5 mm and non-reactive. No signs of neck injury or nuchal rigidity.
Placing a King LT/I-Gel airway and confirming with EtCO2 and bilateral chest rise and auscultation.
Patient is apneic; ventilating with a BVM at 15 liters per minute.
Pulse remains 0, respirations at 8 per minute, patient still in asystolic rhythm.
Blood pressure remains undetectable, oxygen saturation at 0.
Administering another dose of epinephrine intraosseously.
Orogastric tube inserted due to stomach distension after King airway was placed.
EtCO2 is reading at 0.
No signs of pain response or movement.
Patient is known to use insulin and Ciprofloxacin, no allergies reported.
Intubation is being performed with a 7.0 tube.
Confirming tube placement via end-tidal CO2 and bilateral breath sounds, despite epigastric sounds earlier.
End-tidal CO2 remains 0 despite correct tube placement at the mouth.
We are preparing to transport the patient to [COMPANY_NAME] ER under protocol 3-7.
Patient has now been moved to the stretcher and is secured in a supine position.
We are on transport. Administering saline at 15 LPM via IO.
Another 1 mg epi administered via IO per standing order.
End-tidal CO2 remains at 0 after repeated assessments.
ET tube was extubated, OPA placed, and the patient is being ventilated via BVM and mask.
Patient is still apneic, ventilations ongoing at 15 LPM via BVM.
The patient is stable for transport with no sign of ROSC.
Care is being handed off to the ER team as we approach the hospital.
Final update: patient remains in asystole with no spontaneous breathing, secured for arrival.
Final vitals: no pulse, respirations at 8, asystolic with GCS of 3.
Patient has been transferred to Bay Bed 5 for continued resuscitation.
We have received all details-will continue with protocol-guided interventions.
Airway is patent but we require assisted ventilation due to been in a complete unresponsive state.
No abnormal breath sounds are noted at this time.
When King airway was placed, we heard epigastric sounds indicating air entering the stomach.
I note a clean laceration of 15 cm with clear skin edges and no signs of edema or infection.
No blood pressure returned on both upper limbs.
No irregularities of the trachea and neck veins are elevated.
Family states he had last been seen one hour before being found unresponsive.
Intraosseous access placed at left humerus using 45mm IO needle to begin administering fluids.
Rechecking pulse and respiratory rate to track any changes.
Giving another 1 mg of epinephrine as per protocol to try to stimulate circulatory activity.
No gurgling or wheezing heard upon auscultation of chest and back.
No rales, rhonchi or rumbles heard upon auscultation.
No signs of neck rigidity on inspection or while repositioning patient.
Patient is fully immobilized and ready for immediate transport.
No deformities or swelling observed in extremities; skin is warm and dry.
Urine noted on bed, however distension not grossly observable on genital or abdominal exam.
Patient lacks several teeth on both jaws with no recent indications of trauma to mouth.
Checking CO2 readings again, as range is still at 0 despite tube position confirmation.
I see no rashes, petechiae, or purpura on skin during the course of visual exam.
No signs of drainage or hemorrhage in the oropharyngeal or nasal areas.
Readministering epinephrine while squeezing additional fluids via IO site.
Glucose remains stable at 449 mg/dL.
Ongoing zero EtCO2 confirms absence of expiratory CO2 in ventilation, indicating no pulmonary circulation.
Transporting without delay, as no signs of ROSC, based on [COMPANY_NAME] being the only ER around.
Adjusting patient again during transport for movement ease but stability optimal.
Another round of normal saline administered at 250mL to maintain optimal volume.
Administering another Epi dose based on patient not responding prior and protocol recommendations.
Based on EPCR notes, patient is insulin-dependent with no reported previous cardiac events.
We took the king tube out and performed intubation with 7.0 endotracheal tube with Cormack-Lehane view in good field of view.
End-tidal CO2 unobtainable through ET tube as of now.
Extubated and placed oropharyngeal airway to provide higher compliance via bypassing accidental obstruction.
Re-monitoring patient to ensure stable during continued transport.
Patient demographics and medical history included in handwritten ER report.
All performed procedures and administered medications are outlined and time-stamped for documentation.
Transfer of care now in progress with no signs of left-sided weakness or right-side expression deficits.
Patient handed over to ER team who will continue resuscitation in trauma bay 5.
ECG for monitoring suspected hyperkalemia advised during handover.
No reflexes elicited during physical exam; posturing motor response absent.
Scars found on inner thigh from previous surgical procedure.
Re-checked CO2 levels confirm no spontaneous breathing, regardless of airway movement.
BP remains 0, pulse remains undetectable, resp declared at 8 shallow breaths.
Adjusting restraints based on patient size and weight considerations.
Vehicles in first responder transportation mix designated while sirens on as secondary priority.
Patient secured, we were able to fit into ambulance bed without structural concerns.
Final note at time arrived, time of care transition: patient is in stable critical condition.
All protocol steps per 3-7 followed for hyperglycemic state with cardiac arrest signs.
LR taken during transit and time presented at location predefined in patient transfer log.
All patient care completed is within transport pre-hospital scope.
No bruises or bites seen on back, but see past surgical scars bilaterally near hips.
All pain-assessment protocols completed pre-transport and no need for analgesics given unresponsiveness and initial CPR indications.
Bystander reporting of patient's diabetes provided here, with no current SEIZURE or other motor events to include.
EtCO2 monitoring displays 0 throughout despite undetermined EOS insertion.
Family member's distress noted during patient discovery found behind bed area.
Additional trauma screening executed post-handoff per trauma protocol at medical center.
No ligatures or restraints removed during transport, maintained per protocol.
Full disclosure of all trauma points, non-traumatic scene by all accounted crew.
No attempts to decelerate or stop removed from standard transportation procedure.
Noted grooming/lifestyle conditions added in log: unkempt hair, possible infection risk from teeth loss.
No swelling, instability, or narrowing from vocal cord appearance on laryngoscopy.
Sirens on, lights on as part of rapid transport practice.
Final med count verified by team: four epinephrine doses, one grams of NS via IO.
Time noted at arrival aligns with zero CO2 levels upon removal and handover.
Transport time approx. 20 minutes from scene to ER room.
Patient height and estimated weight added to notes and in ER report.
Family members advised not to enter room while patient under transport.
No activity or perception of regaining consciousness or movement upon contact.
Pulses at both carotid arteries absent, confirmed by palpation after collaboration.
No motion or signs of pain upon securing process during ambulance ride.
No codes or ER During ride communicated.
Abdomen is soft, non-tense, without rigidity or guarding.
No sores or dermatitis observed along spinal or hip points.
EPCR signed, family present at hospital with care note provided.
Transport documentation acknowledged and printed for hospital and post-briefing use.
Taking final bag with all provided meds and consumed fluids for reconciliation process.
Bystander mention of insulin bottle located addressing potted scenario.
All interventions and vital signs transferred in digital and paper copies for handover.
No additional resources required for transport; one medically attended and one alternative driver used.
Identification information for family and treating facility/internal communication included in handover package.
Patient entered into hospital system for further treatment and follow-up.
No logistical set-backs for loading, transport or unloading of patient reported.
Transportation duty complete; team returning base for debriefing and vehicle check.
Ongoing assessment monitored pre and post de-escalating emergency services process.
Further action progress in hospital ER after handling and monitoring.
De-activation signoff and all services removed with peace and no further IO Discussion.
EMS protocol finalized and all subsequent steps to be managed by ER staff and their attending.
We will communicate with family about all emergency efforts taken and diagnosis outcome.
